title: Financial goals: from "someday" to a deadline
description: Turn 'I want money' into a plan with a number and a date.
summaryPoints: Sort goals by distance: short-term (under 1 year), medium (1–5), long (5+)
Build SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Spot the opportunity cost — what every purchase quietly costs you later
Balance the split: needs covered, goals fed, some fun always
Launch a goal in 5 steps: pick, plan, track, stick, celebrate

content.title: Money goes where you point it
content.body:
Money is a tool. Use a hammer with no plan and you'll smash a thumb; use money with no goal and it quietly leaks into snacks, rides, and 2 a.m. checkout carts.

Here's the trick: money goes where you tell it to go. Give it no destination and it scatters. A clear goal — with a number and a deadline — is what turns "I want stuff someday" into "I'm getting this, by then."

content.title: Three distances of goal
content.body:
Goals come in three distances:
- **Short-term (under 1 year)** — $80 sneakers, a $150 prom outfit. Quick wins, quick saving.
- **Medium-term (1–5 years)** — a $500 trip with friends, a $3,000 used car. These need a real plan.
- **Long-term (5+ years)** — college, an apartment, even retirement. These shape your life, not just your month.

Watch out: if every dollar chases short-term wins, the long-term dreams never get fed.
content.imageAlt: Winding road with milestone flags marking short, medium, and long-term goals

content.prompt: Goal sorter: how far away is each goal?
If you only chase short-term goals, the long-term ones eventually fund themselves.
content.explanation: Long-term goals only grow if you feed them on purpose. Every dollar that chases quick wins is a dollar college never meets.

content.title: The SMART upgrade
content.body:
"I want money" is a wish. SMART makes it a plan:
- **S**pecific — name it: "a $600 phone"
- **M**easurable — numbers: "$50 a month"
- **A**chievable — realistic for YOUR income
- **R**elevant — it actually matters to you
- **T**ime-bound — deadline: "12 months"

Not SMART: "I want to be rich someday."
SMART: "I'll save $50 a month for 12 months to buy a $600 phone."

Only one of those can actually fail or succeed — and that's exactly why it works.
content.imageAlt: SMART acronym shown as a ladder of five rungs

content.prompt: Decode SMART — match each letter to its meaning.
Which of these is a SMART goal?
options: I want to be rich someday
I'll try to save more money soon
I'll save $50 a month for 12 months to buy a $600 phone
content.explanation: It's specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and has a deadline. The other three can't fail OR succeed — there's nothing to measure.

content.instructions: One word is lying about SMART. Tap it.
content.sentence: The M in SMART means your goal is Manageable.
content.explanation: M is for Measurable — your goal needs numbers ($600 total, $50 a month) so you always know whether you're on track.

content.title: The hidden price tag
content.body:
Every purchase has a price tag you can see — and a hidden one you can't. The hidden one is **opportunity cost**: whatever you gave up by choosing this instead.

Buy $100 sneakers today and you didn't just spend $100 — you un-bought next month's concert tickets. $40 of takeout this week is $40 that never reaches the laptop fund.

The sharpest money question there is: "If I buy this now, what am I losing later?"

content.prompt: What you give up when you choose one thing over another is called ___ cost.
content.explanation: Opportunity cost is the hidden price tag — sneakers today can quietly cost you concert tickets next month.

content.prompt: That $5 school-day snack habit runs about $100 a month. Slide to a full year's damage.
content.explanation: About $100 a month × 12 = $1,200 a year — a whole laptop, eaten one snack at a time.

content.title: Balance (so you don't burn out)
content.body:
If you only save, life feels like punishment. If you only spend, future-you inherits the bill. The move is balance.

Say $100 lands in your pocket:
- $30 covers needs (transport, school stuff)
- $50 feeds a goal
- $20 is guilt-free fun

Your split can look different — the rule is that all three get fed. Then start tiny: one small goal, a weekly amount, somewhere to track it. Win that, and bigger goals stop feeling impossible.

content.prompt: Which choice respects both your goal AND your balance?
content.context: After needs, you have $100 left this month. Your SMART goal: $100 concert tickets in two months — $50 a month.
options: Put $50 toward the tickets, keep $50 for fun and flexibility
Drop the whole $100 on designer shoes you just spotted
Lock all $100 away and have zero fun this month
Skip this month and save $100 next month instead
content.explanation: It feeds the goal exactly on schedule and leaves room to live. The shoes would cost you the concert — opportunity cost in action.

content.prompt: Launch your first goal — put the steps in order.
steps: Pick one short-term goal, like $100 sneakers
Set the weekly amount — $10 for 10 weeks
Track progress in an app, a notebook, or a jar
Stick to the plan — no early raids
Celebrate the win, then aim bigger
content.explanation: Start small on purpose: quick wins build the muscle. Win one goal and the $1,000 ones stop looking impossible.

content.title: Operation Sneakers
content.intro: Your SMART goal: $100 sneakers in 10 weeks — $10 a week. Each choice covers a stretch of the journey. Protect the plan.

steps.situation: Weeks 1–2: your Saturday job pays $25 a week. The plan says $10 a week into the jar.

options.text: Deposit $10 each week, enjoy the rest
options.feedback: Right on plan — and there's still money to live on. Achievable plans are the ones that survive.

options.text: Go hard: save all $25 week one, then nothing
options.feedback: More money, broken rhythm. All-or-nothing plans usually land on 'nothing' by week three.

options.text: Skip the first weeks — you'll catch up later
options.feedback: 'I'll catch up' is the official slogan of goals that never happen.

steps.situation: Weeks 3–5: a hoodie you kind of want hits a flash sale — $30.

options.text: Ask "what does this cost me later?" — then pass
options.feedback: That question is your opportunity-cost radar. Three more deposits land; the sneakers stay on schedule.
You saved $30 these weeks and spent $30 on a maybe-want. Net progress: zero.

options.text: Buy it AND pause saving to recover
options.feedback: The jar went backward and the streak broke. The hoodie's real price keeps growing.

steps.situation: Weeks 6–8: your cousin offers a used gaming headset — $50, "this week only."

options.text: Pass — it's not the goal you chose
options.feedback: R is for Relevant. A deal on something you weren't saving for is just a well-dressed detour.
You saved $30 and spent $50. The 'deal' dragged your goal backward by two whole weeks.

steps.situation: Weeks 9–10: you're $20 from the finish line, and the sneakers just restocked.

options.text: Make the last two deposits, then buy them
options.feedback: Goal: $100. Saved: $100. On time, on plan — celebrate it, then pick the next target.

options.text: Close enough — borrow the last $20 to buy now
options.feedback: Borrowing at the finish line turns a win into a debt. It was only two weeks away.

options.text: Never buy them — keep saving forever
options.feedback: A full jar with no win doesn't build the habit. Celebrating is what makes the next goal easier.

content.outro: Best path: $100 in 10 weeks, zero detours funded by your goal. Pick it, make it SMART, guard it, celebrate it.
